commits.message:
mit-styling-aj1.8.2: shallow focus — foreground snow blooms into bokeh over the masthead

The snow node was the most baseline of the In the Wild siblings: flat
parallax flakes behind a fading masthead. Give it the movement's lens
signature a way the focus node hasn't claimed — shallow depth of field.

Three depth planes now read optically: a deep field of crisp pinprick
flakes, the sharp masthead plane, and — closest to the lens — foreground
flakes thrown so far out of focus they bloom into soft glowing bokeh discs
(bright cat's-eye rim and all) that drift IN FRONT of the title. Nearer
planes drift faster and sway wider, so the parallax reads as real depth.
A lens vignette frames the focal plane.

The defocus is a radial-gradient, not a filter blur, so the soft edge costs
nothing per frame. Distinct from the focus node's chromatic rack — depth of
field, not colour split. Fall stays scroll-coupled; only the decorative sway
and bokeh breathing damp under REDUCE_MOTION.

Verified in Chrome across the node's full scroll range: zero page console
errors, painter fires, scrub maps cleanly, reduced-motion damps sway while
leaving the fall and masthead mapping identical.

mit-styling-aj1.8.1: the rack — the lens hunts plane to plane, each line snapping from a chromatic blur into a crisp word (#39)

The In the Wild title now reads as a true camera rack-focus: each line
resolves on its own staggered slice (near plane, then far), wearing the
lens's chromatic fringe (an RGB channel split) while out of focus and
converging to a sharp word as it snaps in. The colour-split is the
movement's through-the-lens signature, worn by no other painter; it
collapses to zero and the blur softens under prefers-reduced-motion.

The stagger is a value derived from the line count, not a branch, and the
treatment lives entirely in the focus painter + its CSS block — the shared
paint loop stays generic.
